summary: I'm a native of Birmingham, Alabama, and was educated here. Birmingham was once considered one of the worst cities for race relations in the country, especially before 1963. I was involved in the 1963 Birmingham campaign and later served on the city council, working to improve services and representation for all citizens. The city has changed a lot since then, even winning the All-America City Award a few years ago.

I attended Talladega College as part of my education. It was one of the institutions that helped shape my early academic background before I went on to the University of Kansas. Talladega College played an important role in my formative years in Alabama.

After Talladega College, I furthered my education at the University of Kansas. This was a significant step in my academic journey outside Alabama. The University of Kansas helped me gain the skills I needed for my legal and political career.

I was honored to receive an honorary degree from Miles College. This recognition was meaningful as Miles College is a respected institution in Alabama. It reflects my connection to educational institutions that support African American advancement.

Daniel Payne College also awarded me an honorary degree, which I deeply appreciate. Like Miles College, it is an important institution that acknowledges contributions to the community. These honors highlight my involvement in education and civil rights.

Jefferson County is where I began my political involvement, running for the legislature in 1942 to encourage black voter registration. Back then, there were fewer than 1,000 black voters in the entire county, but now there are about 80,000. I also ran for the county executive Democratic committee in the 1950s and helped break down barriers to black political participation here.

My political career has been closely tied to the Democratic Party. I ran for various offices under the party's banner and served on the state executive Democratic committee for two terms. I was also a delegate to the 1968 and 1972 Democratic National Conventions, representing Alabama and working on the credentials committee in 1968. Under Bob Vance's leadership, the state Democratic Party has become more liberal and loyal to the national party.

In 1968, I was appointed to the Birmingham City Council to fill a vacancy and was later elected by a large margin. I served as president pro tempore and chaired the public works committee, where I worked to improve city services like street lighting and traffic engineering. I was proud to represent all citizens fairly and to help increase black representation on the council and city boards.

In 1968, I was a delegate to the Democratic National Convention in Chicago. I was one of only two people from Alabama on the credentials committee, which was a significant responsibility. This experience connected me to national politics during a turbulent election year.

I also served as a delegate to the 1972 Democratic National Convention in Miami. This continued my involvement in national Democratic politics and allowed me to represent Alabama's interests on a larger stage. It was part of my ongoing commitment to the party and civil rights.

During the 1963 demonstrations in Birmingham, I was one of the lawyers representing Martin Luther King Jr. and the groups involved. I also represented him in Montgomery. That year was dangerous for me personally, as my house was bombed twice, but I remained committed to the cause of civil rights alongside Dr. King.

I worked as one of the lawyers representing Martin Luther King Jr. during the civil rights demonstrations in Montgomery. This was part of my broader legal work supporting civil rights cases across Alabama. Montgomery was a key location in the movement, and I was proud to contribute there.

Charles Morgan Jr. was still practicing law in Birmingham during the 1960s, and we worked together on cases, including the lawsuit against election officials that led to an injunction against the state election. We collaborated closely on civil rights legal challenges in Alabama.

I was deeply involved in the 1963 Birmingham campaign, representing those arrested during the demonstrations, including Martin Luther King Jr. Over 3,000 people were arrested, and I served as counsel for many of them. That year was also marked by violent attacks on my home, underscoring the risks we faced.

When a box of 48 sticks of dynamite was found near my home in 1963, demolition experts had to be called from the Anniston Army Depot to deactivate it. They arrived just minutes before the bomb was set to go off, preventing a disaster. This event showed the extreme dangers faced during the civil rights struggle.

The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was a turning point for Birmingham and Alabama. After its passage, the city began to change dramatically from one of the most segregated to a more integrated metropolitan area. It paved the way for greater black participation in politics and city services.

The Voting Rights Act of 1965 helped increase black voter registration in Jefferson County from under 1,000 to about 80,000. This legislation was crucial for expanding political power and representation for African Americans in Alabama. It supported my efforts to encourage voter registration and political involvement.

About three years ago, Birmingham was honored with the All-America City Award. This recognition reflected the city's progress in race relations and civic improvements since the turbulent 1960s. It was a proud moment that showed how far the city had come.

I have been involved with Operation New Birmingham since shortly after 1963 and currently serve on its board and executive committee. This organization worked to address issues like the lack of black police officers and helped establish affirmative action programs in the city. It was a vital part of the civil rights progress in Birmingham.

Bob Vance has been a leader of the Alabama Democratic Party during my involvement, and under his leadership, the party has become fairly liberal and loyal to the national Democratic Party. His leadership has influenced the political landscape in Alabama positively.

I was involved with the Alabama Democratic Conference as part of my broader political and civil rights work. The conference was an important organization for advancing black political participation in Alabama. It worked alongside groups like the Progressive Democratic Council to challenge segregation and discrimination.

The Progressive Democratic Council was another organization I was connected with during my political career. It worked to increase black voter registration and representation in Alabama. Together with the Alabama Democratic Conference, it played a key role in challenging discriminatory practices.

I attended the 1968 Democratic National Convention in Chicago as a delegate from Alabama and served on the credentials committee. This was a significant event during a tumultuous time in American politics, and I was proud to represent my state on the national stage.

In 1972, I was a delegate to the Democratic National Convention held in Miami. This continued my engagement with the national Democratic Party and allowed me to participate in shaping the party's direction during that election cycle.
